title: Cat Drooling: When Excessive Saliva is a Veterinary Emergency
meta_desc: Is your cat drooling? Learn why sudden excessive saliva (ptyalism) is often a high-urgency medical issue and how a photo can help your vet triage.
content_html:
What Is Excessive Drooling in Cats?

Excessive drooling, medically known as ptyalism, occurs when a cat produces more saliva than they can swallow or when they are physically unable to swallow properly. While some cats may produce a small amount of dampness when extremely relaxed or purring, sudden or heavy drooling is generally abnormal for the feline species. It often indicates internal discomfort, oral pain, or a reaction to a toxin.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for sudden excessive drooling is High. Because cats are masters at hiding illness, visible drooling is frequently a sign that a condition has become acute. If the drooling is accompanied by pawing at the mouth, lethargy, foaming, or a foul odor, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. It could indicate anything from a caustic chemical burn or a foreign object stuck in the throat to organ failure or dental abscesses.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of your cat while they are drooling can provide vital clues for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to see the consistency of the saliva (thick, frothy, or watery) and the presence of any blood or discoloration. If you can safely tilt the head back, a photo of the gums or the tongue can help identify ulcers or foreign bodies like sewing needles or string, allowing the clinic to prepare for your arrival more effectively.

faqs.question: Can cats drool just from being happy?
faqs.answer: Some cats do 'happy drool' when purring or being petted, but this is usually a lifelong behavior. If the drooling is new, sudden, or constant, it is likely a medical issue rather than a sign of contentment.

faqs.question: What common household items cause drooling?
faqs.answer: Many common items can cause ptyalism, including toxic plants (like lilies), cleaning chemicals, essential oil diffusers, or even bitter-tasting medications.

faqs.question: Should I try to open my cat's mouth to look inside?
faqs.answer: Only attempt this if your cat is calm and you can do so safely. If the cat is in pain, they may bite reflexively. If you suspect a string or foreign object is stuck, never pull on it, as this can cause internal damage.
